paper_title: Unstable Features, Reproducible Subspaces: Understanding Seed Dependence in Sparse Autoencoders

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used to interpret neural network representations, but their utility depends on whether the learned features are reproducible across training runs. We study this question through \emph{feature stability}: for each SAE feature, we estimate the probability that a similar feature reappears in an independently trained SAE. This yields a scalable per-feature signal that separates stable from unstable features. In a large-scale study across seeds, models, layers, dictionary sizes, and SAE variants, we find a pronounced functional asymmetry: stable features carry most of the reconstruction- and prediction-relevant signal, while unstable features have weak marginal impact and are dominated by low-frequency surface-form triggers in both activation statistics and automatic explanations. Geometrically, unstable features are individually non-reproducible but concentrate in reproducible lower-rank subspaces, suggesting that seed dependence often reflects basis ambiguity within a shared region of activation space rather than pure noise. A controlled synthetic model makes this mechanism explicit, showing that low-rank ground-truth features can be recovered at the subspace level while remaining non-identifiable as individual SAE latents across seeds. Finally, by pooling unique cross-seed features, we construct more stable SAEs while preserving explained variance in this setting. Together, these results show that unstable features are not merely failed or noisy latents: they have weak individual functional impact, but reflect reproducible low-dimensional structure that standard SAEs resolve differently across seeds.

sections.content: 6 2 0 2 0 1 ] . [ 1 8 3 1 2 1 . 6 0 6 2 : r Unstable Features, Reproducible Subspaces: Understanding Seed Dependence in Sparse Autoencoders

sections.title: Daniil Gavrilov
sections.content: T-Tech

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used to interpret neural network representations, but their utility depends on whether the learned features are reproducible across training runs. We study this question through feature stability: for each SAE feature, we estimate the probability that similar feature reappears in an independently trained SAE. This yields scalable per-feature signal that separates stable from unstable features. In large-scale study across seeds, models, layers, dictionary sizes, and SAE variants, we find pronounced functional asymmetry: stable features carry most of the reconstructionand predictionrelevant signal, while unstable features have weak marginal impact and are dominated by low-frequency surface-form triggers in both activation statistics and automatic explanations. Geometrically, unstable features are individually non-reproducible but concentrate in reproducible lower-rank subspaces, suggesting that seed dependence often reflects basis ambiguity within shared region of activation space rather than pure noise. controlled synthetic model makes this mechanism explicit, showing that low-rank ground-truth features can be recovered at the subspace level while remaining nonidentifiable as individual SAE latents across seeds. Finally, by pooling unique cross-seed features, we construct more stable SAEs while preserving explained variance in this setting. Together, these results show that unstable features are not merely failed or noisy latents: they have weak individual functional impact, but reflect reproducible low-dimensional structure that standard SAEs resolve differently across seeds.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are central tool in mechanistic interpretability because they aim to decompose model activations into sparse, humaninterpretable features (Bricken et al., 2023; Cunningham et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2025). basic 1 question, however, is whether these features are reproducible: prior work has shown that SAEs trained on the same activations but with different random seeds can learn substantially different features (Paulo and Belrose, 2025; Leask et al., 2025). This raises finer-grained question: when an individual feature fails to reappear, is the underlying direction absent from the new dictionary, or has the SAE learned different basis for the same region of activation space? This distinction matters because automatic interpretation can assign plausible explanations even in random or non-canonical settings (Heap et al., 2025; Bhalla et al., 2026), so an interpretable-looking feature need not be reproducible unit across runs. We study this question through feature stability: the probability that feature reappears in an independently trained SAE under cosine-similarity matching rule. Using this per-feature signal, we compare stable and unstable features by their functional impact, token structure, automatic explanations, and decoder-space geometry. Our contributions are as follows. Functional asymmetry of stable and unstable features. Stable features carry most of the reconstructionand prediction-relevant signal, whereas unstable features have weak marginal impact and are dominated by low-frequency surface-form triggers in both activation statistics and automatic explanations. Subspace recovery without feature identifiability. Decoder-space analysis shows that unstable features are individually nonreproducible but collectively span reproducible lower-rank subspaces. In controlled synthetic setting, low-rank ground-truth features are likewise recovered at the subspace level while failing to align with individual SAE latents. No stabilityEV trade-off in feature-pool construction. Using deduplicated pool of unique features from multiple runs, we construct SAEs from stable cross-seed features and find that higher explained variance coincides with higher mean feature probability after tuning, suggesting that stability and reconstruction quality need not trade off in this construction setting.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Feature stability. Paulo and Belrose (2025) showed that SAEs trained on the same model and data, differing only in random seed, can learn substantially different feature sets. Leask et al. (2025) argued that SAE latents are not canonical units of analysis, showing that features vary with dictionary size and can be merged across dictionaries. Fel et al. (2025) mitigate instability by anchoring dictionary atoms to data-derived archetypes. Gadgil et al. (2025) propose ensembling independently initialized SAEs via naive bagging and boosting. Our construction of an SAE from unique cross-seed features follows the same bagging intuition, but deduplicates the pooled feature set and selects fixed-size subset of high-stability features rather than simply concatenating all latents from all runs. Chen et al. (2025) improve consistency by assigning features to activation-frequency groups and adapting encoder biases to enforce group-specific target frequencies. Cho et al. (2025) argue that external training data can be partially OOD for the base model and report that training on modelgenerated data improves cross-seed stability. Wang et al. (2025b) impose an ordered latent structure in which increasingly large prefixes of the feature set reconstruct the activation, reducing permutation non-identifiability and improving consistency. Heap et al. (2025) showed that SAEs trained on randomly initialized transformers can still yield plausible automatic interpretations, motivating randommodel controls for SAE interpretability. Bhalla et al. (2026) give complementary geometric account, arguing that multidimensional concepts can admit multiple valid SAE bases, making seeddependent decompositions expected. In this work, we estimate per-feature reappearance probabilities to study instability through seeddependent basis choices within reproducible lowerrank decoder subspaces, probing this view with controlled low-rank synthetic model and comparing stable and unstable features by reconstruction impact, downstream effects, and automatic explanations. Finding similar features across SAEs. Balcells et al. (2024) track how individual SAE features evolve between adjacent layers, while Balagansky et al. (2025b) introduce data-free method for matching SAE features across layers; Laptev et al. (2025) extend this line by constructing crosslayer feature-flow graphs. Wang et al. (2025a) identify corresponding SAE features across different language-model architectures. Anonymous Authors (2026) bridge the stability and matching literatures by benchmarking cross-seed SAE correspondence methods, including cosine and optimaltransport matchers, and evaluating them functionally via ablation effects and substitution tests.

sections.title: 3 Preliminaries
sections.content: 3.1 Sparse Autoencoders sparse autoencoder (SAE) is designed to represent hidden states as sparse linear combination of feature embeddings (Bricken et al., 2023; Cunningham et al., 2023); each coefficient is treated as an activation magnitude, or importance of this feature, which are assumed to be non-negative. Architecturally, an SAE has the following form: = σ(W ench + benc) RF , ˆh = decz + bdec Rd, where is hidden state vector, is vector of feature activation magnitudes, and σ is nonlinear activation function that induces sparsity and non-negativity. SAEs are trained on reconstruction loss plus optional regularization loss that controls sparsity level scaled by coefficient α 0: (1) = ˆh2 2 + αLreg(z). (2) We consider five SAE variants used in our experiments: Vanilla ReLU+ℓ1 SAEs, TopK SAEs, BatchTopK SAEs, HierarchicalTopK SAEs, and JumpReLU SAEs (Cunningham et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2025; Bussmann et al., 2024; Balagansky et al., 2025a; Rajamanoharan et al., 2024). TopK and BatchTopK impose fixed sparsity by retaining only the largest activations, HierarchicalTopK trains one SAE across multiple sparsity budgets, and JumpReLU uses learned per-feature thresholds. For feature in SAE s, we write (s) for the feature, z(s) for its activation, and e(s) Rd for its decoder embedding; decoder columns are unit-normalized after training (Appendix A). 2 3.2 Feature Matching and Stability contain feature with cosine similarity at least θ: Two SAEs trained on similar hidden state distribution (e.g., on the same layer) are expected to converge towards similar representations of the hidden states and therefore similar feature representations. Since SAEs are invariant to feature ordering and two different initializations might produce same dictionaries, but with different indices (Balagansky et al., 2025b), linear assignment problem is usually solved (Paulo and Belrose, 2025; Balagansky et al., 2025b; Fel et al., 2025): given two dictionaries D1 and D2, we seek for permutation matrix Π so that discrepancy between dictionaries D1 and ΠD2 is minimized; this gives one-to-one correspondence. The quality of resulting matching is measure of SAE stability: if it is low, then features are mostly different. This is primary method for previous works on the problem we investigate in this paper. In this work we adopt many-to-one alternative: for each element from D1, we find the most similar element from D2, measured by maximum cosine similarity. This approach is much less computationally expensive and allows to compute stability of individual features, helping us understand how their individual properties correspond to the problem of instability. As robustness check, replacing this argmax-cosine rule with one-to-one Hungarian matching yields nearly identical matched feature sets (IoU = 0.978 0.001).

sections.title: 4 Methodology
sections.content: We study how reliably individual SAE features reappear across random initializations. Throughout, we represent each feature by its decoder vector, and ℓ2-normalize all decoder columns so that cosine similarity reduces to dot product. Feature matching. Given two SAEs }F i=1 and j=1, we say that features and match (cid:1) θ, following (Leask et al., and with decoder columns {e(A) {e(B) }F if cos(cid:0)e(A) 2025), θ = 0.7 unless stated otherwise. , e(B) Reappearance probability. We train + 1 SAEs on identical data and hyperparameters with different random seeds, and choose one run as an anchor (k = 0). For each anchor feature embedding e(0) , we count how many of the other SAEs 3 X0,i = (cid:88) (cid:26) 1 k=1 max j{1,...,F } cos(cid:0)e(0) , e(k) (cid:1) θ (cid:27) . (3) Let (cid:18) pi := Pr max cos(e(0) , e(k) ) θ (cid:19) , = 0. Under independent seeds, X0,i is binomial with parameters and pi, and we estimate ˆpi = X0,i . (4) (cid:80)F Across features, we use the empirical CDF ˆF(p) = 1 i=1 1{ˆpi p}, which converges uniformly to F(p) (GlivenkoCantelli; (Glivenko, 1933; Cantelli, 1933)). Endpoint stability. We focus on endpoint behavior: features that reappear in almost none or almost all runs. Fixing ε = 0.05, we call feature unstable if ˆp(f (0) ) ε and stable if ˆp(f (0) ) 1 ε and use notations ε = {i : ˆp(f (s) (s) ε = {i : ˆp(f (s) (s) ) ε}, ) 1 ε}. Our main task is therefore distilled into finding F(ε) and F(1 ε), via empirical distribution function. Unique feature pool. For the construction experiment, we pool decoder features from several independently trained SAEs and greedily deduplicate them under the same cosine threshold θ = 0.7, producing unique feature pool used to initialize new SAEs (Section 5.3; formal definition in Appendix C.1).

sections.title: 5 Quantitative Analysis
sections.content: Experimental Setup. Unless stated otherwise, we train 96 TopK SAEs (so = 95 comparisons per anchor) on GPT-2 residual-stream activations at layer 7, with TopK = 64 and dictionary size = 214. Figure 1 summarizes the resulting reappearance rates ˆp: many features recur in nearly every run, smaller separated group recurs in almost none, and relatively few lie in the middle (mean ˆp 0.75). Sensitivity to the cosine threshold is reported in Appendix B.2; broader SAE-family comparisons are deferred to Section 7. We now use stability to compare what unstable vs. stable features look like and how much they quency is ωUε 0.0018 (0.18%), compared to ωSε 0.0044 (0.44%) for stable features. Stable features also exhibit heavier high-magnitude tail in µi. (We defer dataset, batching, and exact Ntok details to Appendix B.1.) Token entropy (token diversity). We measure token diversity for feature by the entropy Hi of the empirical distribution over vocabulary IDs at positions where zn,i > 0 (definition in Appendix B.4). Low entropy indicates activation on small token set; high entropy indicates broader lexical support. Both stable and unstable features exhibit two dominant entropy regimes, but with different content. Unstable features range from punctuation/formatting triggers to short subword fragments and brittle substrings, while stable features range from single words or tight synonym clusters to higherlevel concepts with many lexical realizations. Representative interpretations are shown in Figure 2. Automatic interpretability. SAEBench autointerpretation mirrors this split (Appendix E): stable features have higher detection scores (Figure 16), with 4.5 more perfect-score features than unstable ones. Their explanations also differ systematically: unstable features are more often described by surface-form triggers (substrings, capitalization, punctuation), whereas stable features more often describe phrases, syntactic roles, constructions, and broader semantic groupings  (Table 3)  . Keyword frequencies confirm this pattern: substring appears in 38.9% of unstable vs. 11.3% of stable explanations, while phrase rises from 4.1% to 32.0%. GPT-5 predicts stable vs. unstable from explanation text alone with 0.88 accuracy (Appendix E.5). 5. Impact on Reconstruction and Next-Token Loss Frequency-matched masking protocol. To control for the lower activation frequency of unstable features (Section 5.1), for each we mask features sampled uniformly at random from Sε and 4N features sampled uniformly at random from Uε; full sampling, uncertainty, and reweighting details are deferred to Appendix B.5. Explained variance (EV). We measure how much reconstruction quality degrades after masking selected features by evaluating explained variance on the resulting SAE reconstructions (formal definition in Appendix B.5). Figure 3 (left) shows that masking unstable features changes EV only slightly even when masking 4N of them, whereas Figure 1: Feature reappearance across seeds in the main setup (See Section 5). Empirical distribution of reappearance rates ˆp (Eq. 4) for anchor features; the vertical dashed line marks the mean, and error bars indicate variability across 5 anchor choices. Figure 2: Token diversity for stable vs. unstable features. Token entropy Hi with representative feature interpretations. matter. Concretely, we show that: (i) unstable features activate less frequently and (on average) with smaller magnitude tails (as quantified in Section 5.1 and shown in Appendix B.3, Figure 10); (ii) unstable features concentrate on lower-level lexical triggers with lower token diversity (Figure 2); and (iii) even after aggressively masking them, unstable features have much smaller impact on reconstruction and next-token loss than stable features under frequency-matched protocol (Figure 3). All statistics below are computed on held-out token collection; full evaluation and sampling details are deferred to Appendix B. 5.1 Activation Statistics and Token Structure Activation frequency and magnitude. We summarize feature usage with activation frequency ωi (fraction of evaluated token positions where feature activates) and conditional mean magnitude µi (mean activation value conditioned on activation); formal definitions are in Appendix B.3. Appendix B.3 (Figure 10) shows that unstable features activate less frequently on average: their mean fre4 Figure 3: Impact of masking stable vs. unstable features on reconstruction and next-token loss. Left: explained variance (EV) under feature masking. Right: change in next-token loss under activation patching with maskedfeature reconstructions. We mask stable features and 4N unstable features to approximately match expected active mass. Solid vs. dashed curves correspond to using reweighting vs. not using reweighting; full protocol details are deferred to Appendix B.5. masking far fewer stable features yields substantially larger EV drop. This indicates that stable features account for most of the reconstructionrelevant variance. Next-token loss under activation patching. To quantify downstream impact on the base model, we patch the residual stream at the SAE training location with the masked-feature reconstruction and measure the resulting change in next-token cross-entropy, CEbase CEpatched (details in Appendix B.6). As shown in Figure 3 (right), even removing large number of unstable features produces only modest loss change, while masking far fewer stable features yields substantially larger degradation. Overall, unstable features are biased toward lowfrequency surface-form patterns and have limited functional impact, whereas stable features capture most of the reconstructionand prediction-relevant structure. 5.3 Constructing SAE from Unique Features We next test whether stable cross-seed features can initialize seed-robust SAEs. We pool neardeduplicated decoder features from independently seeded SAEs, estimate each pooled features reappearance probability, and construct new = 16,384-latent SAEs from the most probable, least probable, or uniformly sampled pooled features before brief tuning; implementation details and probability histograms are in Sections C.1 and C.2. Figure 4 shows that only modest number of source SAEs is needed: the most-probable construction quickly becomes dominated by highprobability features as the pool grows. After tuning, these dictionaries recover near-baseline explained variance, while least-probable dictionaries remain Figure 4: Unstable-feature fraction in the mostprobable construction as function of the number of source SAEs. As the source pool grows, the selected dictionary contains progressively fewer unstable features and is already dominated by stable ones for modest ensemble sizes. Boxes next to selected points report the size of the pooled unique-feature set and the explained variance of the constructed SAE for that source-pool size. much worse and uniform sampling lies in between (Appendix C.1). Thus stable pooled features can produce seed-robust SAEs without an apparent EV penalty. We further verify (Appendix C.3) that the most-probable construction SAE remains competitive with standard TopK SAE on SAEBench metrics  (Table 2)  , indicating that the pooled construction improves feature stability without sacrificing downstream interpretability performance. Appendix shows that post-training highprobability feature pool shifts some features toward lower reappearance probabilities, suggesting that lower-stability directions can be reconstructionuseful rather than mere random artifacts.

sections.title: 6 Geometric Analysis
sections.content: We now investigate features geometrically at the level of decoder vectors e(s) Rd. Our goal is to show that: (i) unstable features concentrate in lower effective-dimensional subspace than stable features, and (ii) unstable features are linearly separable from the remaining features in decoder space, while the subspace they span exhibits high degree of cross-seed reproducibility. 6.1 Effective Rank of Features Subspaces We compare the dimensionality of the decoder vector subspaces associated with stable and unstable features. Given an index set (s) for unstable features or (s) for stable features), define the submatrix of the decoder (either (s) ε ε ε (s) Iε := (s) dec[:, (s) ε ] Rdms, ms := (s) ε . Using effective rank (ER; Appendix F.1) comand puted on the decoder submatrices (s) Uε (s) , unstable feature sets are consistently lowerSε dimensional: over = 96 seeds, ER/d 0.59 0.65 for unstable sets versus 0.800.81 for stable sets, 2027% reduction  (Table 5)  . Notably, such low-rank regions may reflect known sources of low-dimensional structure in transformers, including self-attention anisotropy and dimensional collapse in attention outputs (Godey et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2026). 6.2 Overlap of Subspaces In this section we show that, although individual unstable features rarely reappear across seeds, the low-rank subspaces spanned by them are reproducible. This suggests that feature instability is primarily driven by seed-dependent mixing within shared subspace, rather than changes in the subspace itself. = 1{i (s) First, we train logistic regression classifier to predict the binary label y(s) ε } from the corresponding decoder vector e(s) Rd. In addition to within-seed evaluation, we test transferability by applying classifier trained on one seed to the decoder vectors of another seed. We evaluate performance using the F1 score (Figure 17) as function of , the number of independently trained SAEs used to estimate the probabilities ˆp(f (s) ). We find that the within-seed and cross-seed F1 scores are very close, and for suffiFigure 5: Explained variance of singular values of decoder submatrices versus SVD rank: within-seed (solid) and cross-seed transfer (dashed). This indicates that the subspaces learned in one seed accurately approximate those in other seeds. ciently large they saturate at approximately 0.73 for ε = 0 and 0.67 for ε = 0.1. Iε Next, to compare decoder vectors across seeds in more detail, we consider the explained variance EV(s) SVD(r) of singular values of matrix (s) (formal definition in Appendix F.1), which measures how well the top-r singular subspace captures the variance within the features set Iε. Using the SVD of (s) Iε and the subspace interpretation of EV(s) SVD(r) (for details, see Appendix F.1), we define the cross-seed analogue EV(as) SVD (r) for = a, which measures how well the top-r singular subspace from seed explains the feature subspace in seed s. Figure 5 compares EV(s) SVD (r), averaged over all choices of and among = 96 seeds, for Uε and Sε with ε {0.01, 0.05}. We find that the top-r singular subspace learned in one seed provides an accurate approximation to the corresponding feature subspaces in other seeds, including for unstable features. SVD(r) to EV(as) 6.3 Controlled Low-Rank Synthetic Model The preceding results suggest that unstable features may reflect basis ambiguity inside shared low-dimensional region of decoder space. To test whether this mechanism is sufficient, we construct synthetic dictionary = (cid:20) (cid:21) , Rnfulld, Rnlowr, Rrd, < d, Rows of are generic full-rank features, while rows of lie in the shared subspace rowspan(V ). We generate activations by sampling 6 evaluated settings, we observe substantial stable subset alongside non-trivial unstable subset, indicating that seed dependence is general property of this training objective rather than one-off artifact. Stability varies across settings: for GPT-2 and Gemma-2, instability generally decreases with depth, while some Pythia settings deviate from this trend. Full endpoint fractions for all configurations are reported in Appendix (Table 6 and Figure 21). Figure 21 shows the full GPT-2 layer-wise trajectory, where stable features increase with depth, unstable features decrease, and next-layer matches occur predominantly among stable features. Effect of sparsity mechanism and reconstruction quality. Table 1 compares SAE variants on GPT-2 layer 7 at fixed = 214, including EV. TopK and BatchTopK are nearly indistinguishable both in stability and EV ( 0.892), suggesting that the dominant source of seed dependence is the hard k-sparsity constraint itself rather than whether it is imposed per-token or per-batch. Within TopK SAEs, increasing improves EV but monotonically increases the unstable fraction and decreases the stable fraction, consistent with stronger sparsity constraints reducing seed-dependent degrees of freedom. Vanilla (ReLU+ℓ1) is extremely stable (near-zero unstable fraction), but at comparable sparsity it has substantially lower EV than TopK, highlighting clear stabilityreconstruction tradeoff when moving along the choice of sparsity/activation mechanism. HierarchicalTopK also improves stability relative to TopK but lowers EV, consistent with additional structure in the sparsity objective reducing seed dependence at some reconstruction cost. JumpReLU improves stability relative to TopK while retaining similar EV at higher sparsities, suggesting that different sparsity mechanisms can shift this stabilityreconstruction balance in different ways. More SAE training does not remove instability. Figure 7 shows the unstable fraction in the main TopK setting as function of total SAE training tokens from 10M to 10B. The unstable fraction decreases early in training and then approaches clear non-zero plateau ( 8% at 10B tokens in this setting). In contrast, the stable fraction continues to increase with additional training but with diminishing returns; we report the corresponding curve and full setup details in Appendix  (Fig. 22)  . Dead-salmon baseline: trained vs. random transformers. Finally, we repeat the same SAE training procedure on activations from randomly Figure 6: Synthetic low-rank model (d = 32, = 2, = 8). The first 80 ground-truth features are full-rank features and the last 20 lie in shared rank-2 subspace. Full-rank features (blue) have near-perfect cross-seed reappearance probability and cosine similarity to their matched ground-truth features, whereas low-rank features (red) do not. dictionary rows, summing them with unit coefficients, and training TopK SAEs from multiple seeds. Full-rank features should be individually identifiable, whereas low-rank features may only be identifiable up to rotations or mixtures within the shared span. Figure 6 confirms this prediction for = 32, = 2, = 8: full-rank features have near-perfect reappearance probability and cosine similarity to matched ground-truth features, while low-rank features have much lower reappearance probability ( 0.25) and worse one-to-one recovery. Appendix F.2 shows the same qualitative split in additional settings and further shows that the learned low-rank block has lower effective rank and non-random cross-seed subspace similarity. Thus, the empirical geometry of unstable SAE features can arise naturally from seed-dependent basis choices within reproducible low-rank subspace, rather than from the absence of shared structure. Appendix F.2.1 gives an additional residual-based diagnostic that partially recovers the low-rank ground-truth atoms by clustering examples with one active unstable feature.

sections.title: 7 Other Setups
sections.content: We next test whether the stability patterns from the main setting persist under changes to the base model, layer, and dictionary size, and under alternative SAE nonlinearities, training budgets, and random-model null baseline. Overall, these ablations show that non-trivial unstable subset coexists with large stable subset across settings. Across base models, layers, and dictionary sizes. We first train TopK SAEs across several base models, layers, and dictionary sizes. Across all 7 SAE type Sparsity EV Unstable Stable Vanilla (ReLU + ℓ1) TopK BatchTopK HierarchicalTopK JumpReLU 62 91 32 48 64 80 64 64 29 58 0.833 0.856 0.862 0.882 0.892 0.902 0.892 0.876 0.841 0.884 0.902 0.003 0. 0.053 0.080 0.098 0.118 0.097 0.028 0.049 0.034 0.038 0.810 0.764 0.619 0.560 0.522 0. 0.523 0.716 0.624 0.620 0.602 Table 1: SAE type comparison on GPT-2 at layer 7 with = 214. Figure 7: Unstable fraction vs. SAE training tokens. Fraction of unstable features in the main TopK setting as function of total SAE training tokens. initialized GPT-2 (same architecture and SAE hyperparameters). Our stability metric sharply distinguishes these cases: Figure 8 compares stability as function of the cosine matching threshold θ for SAEs trained on trained GPT-2 versus random GPT-2. Across wide range of θ, the trained-model SAEs retain large stable subset, whereas in the random-model setting the stable fraction collapses and instability dominates. Importantly, automatic interpretation alone would not reliably flag this failure mode: SAEs trained on the random model can still achieve high detection scores (Appendix G.3, Fig. 23). Together, these results show why stability is useful alongside automatic interpretation: plausible explanations can arise even when features are not reproducible across seeds.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: Our results show that SAE seed dependence is structured rather than arbitrary. Feature stability, measured via cross-run reappearance probability, separates learned features into two empirically distinct regimes: stable features carry most of 8 Figure 8: Dead-salmon control: stability on trained vs. random transformers. Fractions of stable and unstable features as function of cosine matching threshold θ, comparing SAEs trained on trained GPT-2 versus randomly initialized GPT-2 (same architecture and SAE setup). the reconstructionand prediction-relevant signal and more often correspond to structural or compositional patterns, while unstable features have weaker marginal impact and are biased toward lowfrequency surface-form triggers. Crucially, unstable features are not merely failed or noisy latents. Although individual unstable features rarely reappear across seeds, they concentrate in reproducible lower-rank subspaces, suggesting that seed dependence often reflects different basis choices within shared region of activation space. Our controlled toy model makes this mechanism explicit: low-rank ground-truth features can be recovered at the subspace level while remaining non-identifiable as individual SAE latents across seeds. Taken together, these results show that single SAE dictionary can obscure important cross-seed structure. They also show that cross-seed structure can be reused: by pooling high-probability features across runs and briefly post-training, we construct more seed-robust dictionaries without sacrificing reconstruction quality. natural direction for future work is to identify the model components that give rise to these reproducible low-rank components, and to develop methods for recovering identifiable individual features within them.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: First, the stable and unstable sets depend on two threshold choices: the decoder-cosine matching threshold θ for cross-seed recurrence and the endpoint cutoff ε for selecting features that reappear almost never or almost always. In the main experiments we use θ = 0.7 and ε = 0.05. Our threshold analyses indicate that the main qualitative trends are robust to reasonable variations of both, but the precise sizes and membership of the endpoint sets remain conditional on these choices. Second, our low-rank subspace analysis should be interpreted as evidence for concrete mechanism of seed dependence, rather than as complete identification of the mechanism generating instability in real LLM SAEs. The empirical results and synthetic model show that individually unstable features can arise from basis ambiguity within reproducible lowerrank subspaces, but they do not prove that every unstable feature in real models is produced by this mechanism. Third, our no stabilityEV tradeoff result is specific to the feature-pool construction, which uses features aggregated from several independently trained SAEs. Thus, it shows that the trade-off can be avoided in this construction setting, but not that single SAE training objective can achieve the same stability profile at no reconstruction cost.

sections.title: A SAE Training Details
sections.content: A.1 SAE variants Vanilla SAE (Cunningham et al., 2023) uses ReLU as the nonlinearity and Lreg = z1. TopK SAE (Gao et al., 2025) imposes sparsity on by zeroing out all entries outside the top-k values. Batch TopK SAE (Bussmann et al., 2024) applies the same idea at the batch level, zeroing out elements below the top-(batch size k) threshold; these activations fix the desired sparsity level and remove the need for an explicit regularization term. HierarchicalTopK SAE (Balagansky et al., 2025a) trains single dictionary across nested sparsity budgets, so increasingly large prefixes of the selected features are optimized to reconstruct the activation. JumpReLU SAE (Rajamanoharan et al., 2024) modifies the Vanilla SAE by learning threshold θi for each feature and using JumpReLU(x) = xH(x θ), where is the Heaviside function. A.2 SAE training setup Unless otherwise stated, each SAE is trained on 1B token activations sampled from the sample-10BT split of FineWeb. We train all seeds with the same activation sampling order: at each optimization step, we maintain an activation store containing 8192 32 token activations and sample 8192 token positions uniformly at random from this store. The random seed controlling this sampling procedure is shared across SAE runs, so differences between runs are due to SAE initialization only. We use an auxiliary loss for dead features, following Gao et al. (2025). feature is marked as dead if it has not activated for more than 20 consecutive batches. The auxiliary loss gives such dead features learning signal by asking them to help reconstruct the residual error left by the main active features. Concretely, after computing the standard SAE reconstruction ˆx, we form the residual = ˆx. The auxiliary loss selects dead features with large activations on the current batch and uses their decoder directions to reconstruct this residual. This encourages inactive features to specialize to currently unexplained directions, reducing the chance that they remain permanently unused. The auxiliary term is then added to the standard SAE training objective during training. 10 A.3 Decoder normalization During training, we normalize decoder weights so that feature embeddings and gradients have unit norm. Namely, suppose that wi and gi is decoder column and its gradient correspondingly, then the procedure is: 1. Compute row-normalized decoder weights: ui = wi wi . 2. Remove the component parallel to ui from the gradient: gi gi (gT ui)ui, so that gradient step will be performed on the tangent space of the unit sphere. Figure 9: Endpoint fractions vs. cosine threshold θ. Fractions of stable and unstable features as function of cosine matching threshold θ for several SAE types in the same base-model setting. 3. Set weights to normalized: wi ui. After training, we fold the mean and the standard deviation of the initial hidden state buffer into the weights so that no explicit normalization of the input and rescaling of the output is required, then normalize the decoder and scale encoder weights and bias to counteract this normalization, so that SAE do not change the output.

sections.title: Analysis
sections.content: B.1 Evaluation tokens and activation collection All quantitative statistics (frequencies, magnitudes, entropy, EV and CE under patching) are computed over held-out collection of token positions. Let Beval be the number of evaluation batches and the context length, and define Ntok = Beval T. For token index {1, . . . , Ntok}, let hn Rd denote the base-model activation at the SAE training location (residual stream), and let zn RF 0 denote SAE feature activations. We use the SAE reconstruction ˆhn = deczn + bdec. (5) All evaluation batches are disjoint from SAE training batches. B.2 Cosine-threshold sensitivity across SAE families We vary the cosine matching threshold θ used to declare cross-seed feature matches and recompute the endpoint fractions for each SAE family in the main GPT-2 setting. Figure 9 shows that increasing θ makes matching stricter, so the stable fraction falls and the unstable fraction rises across all SAE types. The qualitative ordering is preserved throughout the sweep: TopK and BatchTopK remain nearly identical, JumpReLU stays intermediate, and Vanilla exhibits the strongest endpoint stability. B.3 Usage statistics: activation frequency and mean magnitude We quantify how often each feature is used and how strongly it fires when active via: ωi = µi = Ntok(cid:88) 1{zn,i > 0}, 1 Ntok n=1 (cid:80)Ntok n=1 zn,i n=1 1{zn,i > 0} . (cid:80)Ntok (6) (7) Here ωi is the fraction of evaluated token positions where feature activates, and µi is the mean activation magnitude conditioned on activation. Figure 10 visualizes these two statistics jointly for stable and unstable features. B.4 Token entropy computation Let vn {1, . . . , V} be the vocabulary token id at token position n. For each feature i, define tokenconditional activation counts and the induced token distribution ci(v) = Ntok(cid:88) n=1 1{(vn = v) (zn,i > 0)}, (8) πi(v) = ci(v) vV ci(v) (cid:80) , and token entropy (9) Hi = (cid:88) vV πi(v) log πi(v). (10) 11 SAE reconstruction ˆhn at each token position: (M ) = ˆh (M ) ˆhn2 (M ) 2 + δ ˆh , (14) where δ > 0 is small constant for numerical stability. B.6 Next-token loss under activation patching To measure downstream impact, we patch the residual stream at the SAE training location. For each token position n, we run clean forward pass and compute cross-entropy CEbase on the evaluation tokens. We then rerun the forward pass (M ) where hn is replaced by either ˆh ing) or (with reweighting), and compute CEpatched(M ) on the same evaluation tokens. We report (no reweight- (M ) CE(M ) = CEbase CEpatched(M ), (15) so more negative values indicate larger degradation (as in Figure 3).

sections.title: C Additional Details for Constructed and
sections.content: Stable-Pooled SAEs C.1 Explained variance during tuning of constructed SAEs To test feature-pool construction, we train collection of independently seeded SAEs and merge their decoder features into the unique pool U, so near-duplicate cross-seed features appear only once. Formally, we process all features from source SAEs in lexicographic order. Let = (s 1)F + , and write e(ct) = e(s) index candidate ct = (s) . Starting with U(0) = , define U(t) = U(t1) {ct}, U(t1), e(ct)e(f ) < θ, max U(t1) otherwise. (16) where the maximum over the empty set is . The final pool is = U(SF ), and we use the same threshold θ = 0.7 as in feature matching. For source-pool size n, we form the pool from the first source SAEs and estimate each pooled features empirical reappearance probability ˆp within that subset. We then instantiate new SAEs with = 16,384 latents using three feature-selection rules: the most probable pooled features, the least probable pooled features, and an equiprobable baseline that samples pooled features uniformly regardless of ˆp. Figure 10: Activation frequency and conditional mean magnitude for stable vs. unstable features. Unstable features are concentrated at lower frequencies, while stable features exhibit heavier high-magnitude tail. We use the natural logarithm. Features that never activate on the evaluation set are excluded from entropy plots. B.5 Masking protocol, uncertainty, and reweighting For masked index set {1, . . . , }, define z(M ) (M ) ˆh = zn (1 1M ), = decz(M ) + bdec. (11) (12) We measure reconstruction quality by explained variance EV(M ) = 1 En (M ) (cid:104) hn ˆh (cid:2)hn h2 En 2 2 2 (cid:3) (cid:105) , = En[hn]. (13) For each masking budget , we sample masks as follows: Sample MS by drawing features uniformly without replacement from Sε. Sample MU by drawing 4N features uniformly without replacement from Uε. We repeat this sampling procedure = 10 times independently for each . Plots report the mean across these draws, and the shaded band indicates one standard deviation across draws. Reweighting. In addition to direct masked re- (M ) constructions ˆh , we evaluate reweighted variant that matches the ℓ2 norm of the unmasked 12 Figure 11: Explained variance during brief tuning of SAEs constructed from different feature subsets. Initialization from the most probable features recovers nearly the same explained variance as the original SAE, the equiprobable construction performs somewhat worse, and the least-probable construction lags substantially behind. Metric Standard Most-probable Sparse Probing (Top-1) AutoInterp (mean) SCR (Top 20) TPP (Top 20) 0.656 0.818 0.269 0.107 0.673 0.850 0.258 0.090 Table 2: SAEBench metrics for standard and mostprobable construction SAEs. All constructed SAEs are briefly tuned for approximately 2M tokens before evaluation. Figure 11 reports the explained variance during this brief tuning for SAEs initialized from the mostprobable, equiprobable, and least-probable feature subsets. The least-probable construction remains below even randomly initialized SAE trained for the same token budget, whereas the most-probable and equiprobable constructions reach essentially the same EV as the post-trained baseline SAE. C.2 Probability distributions of selected features in constructed SAEs Figure 12 shows the reappearance-probability distributions induced by the most-probable, equiprobable, and least-probable construction rules across different source-pool sizes. C.3 SAEBench metrics for standard and most-probable construction SAEs Table 2 shows that the most-probable construction SAE remains broadly competitive with standard TopK SAEs on SAEBench: SCR and TPP decrease modestly, while Sparse Probing Top-1 and the Autointerp score slightly improve. 13 Figure 12: Probability distributions of selected features for different source-pool sizes. Rows correspond to the most-probable, equiprobable, and least-probable constructions; columns use = 4, 10, and 96 source SAEs. Even small source pools already make the mostprobable construction concentrate on high-reappearance features, while the least-probable construction remains dominated by near-zero-probability features. C.4 Automatic interpretation for baseline vs. most-probable-feature SAE Figure 13 compares detection-score distributions for the baseline SAE and the SAE constructed from the most probable feature pool.

sections.title: D Additional Discussion on
sections.content: Stability-Inducing Methods Our main analysis studies what unstable features are like and why they arise under fixed SAE objective. related but distinct question is how to make learned SAE dictionaries more stable. We group stability-inducing approaches into three broad axes: explicit or implicit regularization, metric reweighting, and bagging-style feature pooling. Regularization and architectural constraints. Regularization or additional constraints can reduce the space of near-equivalent solutions and thereby increase cross-seed agreement. In our experiments, Vanilla ReLU+ℓ1 SAEs are substantially more stable than TopK SAEs at comparable sparsity, but have lower EV. JumpReLU SAEs also include an ℓ1-style sparsity regularization term, which may partly explain their improved stability relative to TopK. Archetypal SAEs constrain the optimization by tying learnable features to archetypes derived from training data (Fel et al., 2025); we do not include them in our quantitative comparison because the original method was evaluated on vision models, and in our text-activation experiments we were Figure 13: Automatic interpretation scores: baseline SAE vs. SAE constructed from the most probable features. Histogram of detection scores; dashed lines show mean scores for each SAE. unable to obtain competitive reconstruction performance. HierarchicalTopK is different: it does not add an explicit ℓ1 penalty, but can be viewed as an implicit regularizer because single dictionary must support good reconstruction across nested sparsity budgets (Balagansky et al., 2025a). This may reduce the degeneracy of the fixed-k TopK objective and make seed-dependent basis choices less likely. This is also consistent with the TopK sparsity sweep in Table 1: lower yields lower EV but higher stability, suggesting that stronger sparsity constraints can reduce seed-dependent basis choices. Data whitening and Mahalanobis losses. Our results show that unstable features contribute less to reconstruction, suggesting that they may lie in directions that are downweighted by the standard MSE objective. This motivates reweighting the reconstruction metric. Let Rd be hidden state, ˆx its SAE reconstruction, = ˆx the residual, and Σ Rdd the activation covariance. Standard MSE uses rr, while regularized Mahalanobis loss uses r(Σ + λI)1r. This is closely related to training on whitened activations (Saraswatula and Klindt, 2025). To interpolate continuously between MSE and this Mahalanobis objective, we train with r(Σ + λI)αr, α [0, 1], where α = 0 recovers MSE and α = 1 recovers regularized Mahalanobis loss. Figure 14 shows that increasing α can reduce instability, but substantially worsens EV. This is expected: reweighting Figure 14: Routes to SAE stability in the EV instability plane. Orange points compare SAE architecture/objective variants, including TopK, JumpReLU, Vanilla, and HierarchicalTopK. Green points vary the Mahalanobis/whitening interpolation for TopK SAEs. Blue points show most-probable feature-pool SAEs after post-training with different numbers of source SAEs; rightmost blue point corresponds to the post-trained TopK SAE (i.e. feature pool with single SAE). Architectural and loss-level changes can reduce instability but trade off against EV, whereas feature pooling can reach low instability while preserving high EV. low-variance directions makes the training objective less aligned with standard reconstruction quality. Constructing SAEs from feature pools. third route is to aggregate reproducible structure across runs rather than changing the single-run objective. Our most-probable feature-pool construction deduplicates decoder features across independently trained SAEs, selects high-probability features, and briefly post-trains the resulting dictionary. As shown in Figure 14, this approach can substantially reduce instability while preserving high EV. Interestingly, before post-training the selected features all have reappearance probability bounded away from zero (around 0.3 in the shown run), but after post-training some features shift toward lower reappearance probabilities (Figure 15). This supports the view that lower-stability directions can be useful for reconstruction, rather than being mere random artifacts.

sections.title:
E Automatic Interpretation and
Qualitative Feature Analysis
sections.content: This appendix contains the detailed qualitative results summarized in the token-entropy discussion of Section 5. We compare stable and unstable fea14 Figure 15: Feature reappearance probabilities before and after post-training. The most-probable construction initializes from relatively high-probability pooled features, but post-training shifts some features toward lower reappearance probabilities. Lower-stability directions can therefore be reconstruction-useful rather than mere random artifacts. tures using automatic feature interpretation and explanation text analysis. E.1 Qualitative results from automatic interpretation We use the same stable/unstable split as elsewhere in the paper (with ε = 0.05 and single anchor SAE). We run the SAEBench autointerpretation pipeline (Karvonen et al., 2025) with Qwen/Qwen3-32B as the interpreter/evaluator model; full settings are given below in Appendix E.2. Stable features are more interpretable. SAEBench assigns each feature detection score, defined as the evaluator models accuracy at predicting whether given context contains any token position on which the feature activates (Appendix E.3). Figure 16 shows the distribution of detection scores for stable and unstable features. Stable features achieve noticeably higher scores overall. This gap is driven primarily by the mass at score = 1 (perfect detection on held-out contexts): in our setting, the fraction of stable features with score 1 is 4.5 larger than for unstable features. Explanations differ systematically. Inspecting the generated explanations reveals consistent qualitative split. Unstable features are frequently described in terms of surface form: letter patterns, capitalization, prefixes, punctuation, and short substrings. Stable features more often describe how words function in context: phrases and construcFigure 16: Automatic interpretation (detection) scores for stable vs. unstable features. Stable features achieve higher scores, with substantially larger mass at score = 1. tions, syntactic roles (e.g., pronouns, conjunctions), or broader semantic groupings. This aligns with Section 5, where unstable features concentrate on lower token-entropy regimes dominated by punctuation/subword triggers, while stable features extend to higher-entropy lexical and conceptual patterns. Table 3 provides representative examples illustrating this surface-form vs. structural distinction. Keyword evidence for surface vs. structural patterns. To quantify the surface-vs-structural distinction in explanation text, we measure how often explanations contain keywords such as substring, starting, letter (surface-form) versus phrase, expression, construction (structural/compositional), across bins of ˆp(f (0) ). We restrict to explanations with detection score > 0.7 to reduce noise from low-quality explanations. simple summary already shows large separation: in the unstable features explanations, substring appears in 38.9% of explanations, while for the stable ones it appears in 11.3%; conversely, phrase increases from 4.1% to 32.0% from unstable to stable features. The full table (with confidence intervals and bin counts) is given in Appendix E.4. Explanations alone predict stability. As an auxiliary test, we ask frontier LLM to classify features as stable vs. unstable using only their autointerp explanations, with labels anonymized during prompting. On balanced evaluation set, the model achieves 0.88 accuracy. Full prompts and the exact protocol are given in Appendix E.5. 15 Unstable features Stable features words starting with ve followed by letter that continues root or morpheme proper nouns, especially those starting with capital letters the substring Tor at the start of words words starting with Div or containing the substring div in various contexts the substring wr at the start of words the substring Tro at the start of words words starting with the substrings wa, por, po, or similar letter combinations partial words with double letters in the middle, such as Perr, Phys, Ol, Bonni, feat, iodin, and Coleman words containing the substring cran followed by letter, often related to cranberries or cranium. first names, especially when preceded by description or title words starting with Rum, mist, and of as well as variations like Rumor, misten, and of words containing the substring br followed by on or in medical or anatomical contexts words starting with El or el surnames and partial surnames, especially those starting with Hart the word batch and similar capitalized substrings like Dow and Alabama names or parts of names with specific stylistic or phonetic pattern, particularly those beginning with Alfred, styl, transc, or University words related to storage containers, especially tank, tanks, and variations like gallons, empty, and tankers. words starting with pie or piez and related technical or compound terms the surname Gordon and related proper nouns words starting with the substring Whit or Wal government-related terms such as Cabinet, Prime, and bills. words related to substances, fluids, or materials in contexts of waste, water, or bodily fluids capitalized names and the substring Marshall with variations the phrase so as to and similar constructions indicating purpose or intent reflexive pronouns and words referring to oneself or others technical terms related to servers, connections, and computing components phrases involving positional prepositions like in the, right up to, and next to. phrases indicating reference to previously mentioned information, such as as described above or mentioned earlier phrases describing characters and their roles in stories or films words related to peace and calmness, including variations like peaceable and peacefully the word firm and similar capitalized terms in business or organizational contexts words starting with the letter (case-insensitive) words ending in the substring -el the word lock and variations with attached punctuation or nearby words the phrase no longer and related negated continuations names and parts of names starting with Mc or McK or Mac words related to negative outcomes or effects, especially when paired with terms like impact, effects, or feedback. words indicating uncertainty or hypothetical situations, such as potential, possible, and similar variations phrases involving change with quantity or degree, especially those using the. . . the. . . or increases/decreases with. the word healthy and related concepts about diet and well-being variations of the word dig and related actions or contexts adjectives and phrases starting with unthat convey sense of freedom or lack of restriction coordinating conjunctions and and or and their surrounding context words related to teenagers and specific substrings like teen, teens, Teen, and agers the word course and related educational concepts language names and related terms such as English, Spanish, Arabic, and their variations Table 3: Representative automatic interpretations. Examples of unstable surface-form explanations and stable structural/compositional explanations. Only interpretations with detection score > 0.7 are shown. E.2 Auto-interpretation SAEBench setup We use the SAEBench auto-interpretation pipeline (Karvonen et al., 2025) with Qwen/Qwen3-32B as both interpreter and evaluator model. Interpretation/evaluation contexts are drawn from held-out slice of FineWeb consisting of 2M tokens. For each feature, we build prompt contexts from token windows around activations and cap the models generation length at 128 tokens. Example selection. For each feature, we construct two disjoint sets of contexts: Explanation set (used to generate the explanation): 10 contexts with the largest activation value, plus 5 additional contexts sampled via importance sampling. Evaluation set (used to score the explanation): 2 additional top-activation contexts (not overlapping with the explanation set), plus 10 importance-sampled contexts, plus 2 random contexts. All sampling is performed independently per feature. The resulting prompts follow SAEBench defaults, aside from the local inference adjustments described above. E.3 Detection score definition Given feature and context window (a sequence of tokens), define the binary label yi(c) = 1{ token position in such that zt,i > 0}, i.e., whether the context contains any position where the feature activates. Given the interpreterproduced explanation text Ei, the evaluator model predicts ˆyi(c) {0, 1}. The detection score for feature is the accuracy on the held-out evaluation set: DSi = 1 Ci (cid:88) cCi 1{ˆyi(c) = yi(c)}, where Ci is the evaluation context set described in Appendix E.2. 16 E.4 Keyword analysis across stability bins ... We bin features by their empirical reappearance rate ˆp(f (0) ) and compute, for each bin and each keyword k, the empirical fraction ˆq of explanations that contain as substring match (case-sensitive, using the raw explanation text). To reduce noise from low-quality explanations, we restrict to features with detection score DSi > 0.7. We report confidence intervals for each proportion. Below we include two versions of the same table: Wald interval (symmetric; matches the common ˆq 1.96(cid:112)ˆq(1 ˆq)/n form) and Wilson interval (typically better calibrated near 0/1). Table 4 reports the resulting keyword frequencies with Wald confidence intervals. E.5 LLM-only classification from explanation text We test whether the auto-interp explanation text alone contains enough signal to predict feature stability. Concretely, we run blind classification experiment where frontier LLM (GPT-5) receives only short natural-language explanations produced by the auto-interpretation pipeline summarized in Appendix and must predict binary label. No activation traces, token examples, decoder vectors, or ˆp values are shown. Data and split. We construct balanced dataset of explanations from endpoint features with ε = 0.05 using the same anchor SAE as in the main text. We sample 50 explanations from each class for in-context training (100 total) and disjoint 50 explanations from each class for evaluation (100 total). During prompting, labels are anonymized as {0, 1} and the model is not told what the labels correspond to. Classification prompt. We use single incontext learning prompt: first provide labeled training examples (balanced across labels), then ask for labels on the test set, requesting one label per line and no additional text. We report accuracy on the balanced test set. Prompt A: LLM-only classification template You will see short explanations of patterns that activate feature. Each explanation has label 0 or 1. Learn what distinguishes the labels. TRAINING EXAMPLES: Label: 0 Explanation: <EXPLANATION_1> Label: 0 Explanation: <EXPLANATION_2> ... Label: 1 Explanation: <EXPLANATION_51> Label: 1 Explanation: <EXPLANATION_52> Now predict labels for the TEST explanations. Output one label (0 or 1) per line, in order, with no extra text. TEST EXPLANATIONS: 1) <TEST_EXPLANATION_1> 2) <TEST_EXPLANATION_2> ... Result. On the balanced test set (50 explanations per class), GPT-5 achieves accuracy 0.88. Since the model sees only explanation text, this indicates that stability is strongly reflected in the linguistic descriptions produced by auto-interpretation. Prompt B: Qualitative class-summary template Here are explanations from two classes (A and B). Summarize typical patterns in each class. Be concise and concrete. Class A: - <EXPLANATION_A1> - <EXPLANATION_A2> ... Class B: - <EXPLANATION_B1> - <EXPLANATION_B2> ... Qualitative class summaries prompt. In addition to classification, we ask the same LLM to summarize typical patterns in each class, using explanations only. We provide small random subset from each class (without mentioning stability) and request concise descriptions. Model-produced summaries. The model consistently describes the two classes in terms of surface-form versus structural/compositional language. In representative run it summarized: Unstable: explanations emphasize what the word looks like on the surface (substrings, prefixes, capitalization, punctuation) and narrow lexical triggers. Stable: explanations emphasize how words function in sentences (phrases, constructions, discourse roles) and broader compositional or semantic groupings. Notes and limitations. This experiment is intended as lightweight diagnostic rather than primary result. Because it relies on particular evaluator model and prompt format, we treat it as supportive evidence that the surface-vs-structural distinction is present in explanation text, consistent with Table 3 and the keyword analysis in Appendix E.4. Keyword [0.00, 0.05) (n=1020) [0.05, 0.10) (n=220) [0.90, 0.95) (n=1053) [0.95, 1.00] (n=6547) substring starting letter phrase expression construction 0.3890.030 0.2760.027 0.1080.019 0.0410.012 0.0100.006 0.0080.005 0.2090.054 0.0820.036 0.0680.033 0.2050.053 0.0360.025 0.0320.023 0.1210.020 0.0500.013 0.0360.011 0.3130.028 0.0560.014 0.0660.015 0.1130.008 0.0520.005 0.0370.005 0.3200.011 0.0580.006 0.0600.006 Table 4: Keyword frequencies in feature explanations across reappearance bins (Wald 95% CI). Each entry reports the fraction of explanations containing the given keyword, shown as ˆq (95% CI). Column headers give bin ranges for ˆp(f (0) ) and the number of features in that bin after restricting to detection score > 0.7. Figure 17: F1 score for classification separating unstable features from all others. Solid curves report within-seed evaluation, while dashed curves report transfer of the classifier to different seed.

sections.title: Analysis
sections.content: Figure 17 provides the full F1 curves for the linearseparability classifier discussed in the main text. To fairly compare the effective ranks of submatrices with different numbers of features, we calculate them under size-matched protocol: for each seed for all feature sets we select k(s) = min (cid:0)U (s) 0.05(cid:1), and for sets whose number of features differs from k(s), we estimate ER by averaging over = 50 random subsamples of size k(s). 0.01, (s) 0.05, (s) 0.01, (s) We define the explained variance EV(s) SVD(r) of singular values of matrix (s) Iε as EV(s) SVD(r) = (cid:88) i= (cid:0)σ(s) (cid:1)2/ min(d,ms) (cid:88) i=1 (cid:0)σ(s) (cid:1)2. ε Let (s) [:, 1 : r] Rdr denotes the matrix, whose columns are the top-r left singular vectors, and define the projector onto their span . (b) = 32, = 1, = 8. (c) = 32, = 4, = 4. Figure 18: Additional synthetic settings. The same full-rank versus low-rank split appears across multiple values of the subspace rank and sparsity level k: fullrank features retain high reappearance probabilities and high cosine similarity to ground truth, whereas low-rank features do not. Intuitively, EV(as) SVD (r) measures how well the topr singular subspace learned in seed explains the feature subspace in seed s. F.2 Additional Results for the Controlled Low-Rank Synthetic Model We report additional synthetic-model results beyond the main setting from Section 6.3. Across multiple choices of low-rank dimension and sparsity level k, the same qualitative pattern persists: full-rank features are consistently stable and well recovered, while features confined to the shared low-rank block are much less stable as individual directions. Figure 18 shows these additional settings. Figure 19 reports the corresponding effectiverank and cross-seed subspace-similarity diagnostics. Figure 19: Effective rank and cross-seed subspace similarity in the synthetic model (d = 32, = 2, = 8). The learned low-rank block has smaller effective rank than the full-rank block, yet its cross-seed subspace similarity remains clearly above the random baseline. Thus the low-rank features are unstable as individual vectors but still reconstruct reproducible shared subspace. F.2.1 Residual-Based Diagnostic for Low-Rank Feature Recovery The toy model also admits diagnostic for checking whether the low-rank ground-truth atoms are present in the residual structure, even when they are not recovered as individual SAE latents. Let an activation with exactly one low-rank ground-truth feature be h(t) = (cid:88) iFt fi + gr(t), where fi are full-rank ground-truth features and gr(t) is low-rank ground-truth feature. Suppose the SAE reconstruction activates the correctly identified full-rank features and exactly one unstable feature ˆgj(t): ˆh(t) (cid:88) iFt fi + ˆgj(t). Then the residual-corrected vector h(t) ˆh(t) + ˆgj(t) gr(t). Let z(t) denote the vector of unstable-feature activations for example t. Then = (cid:111) (cid:110) h(t) ˆh(t) + ˆgj(t) : z(t)0 = . should cluster around the ground-truth low-rank atoms g1, . . . , gM , assuming each low-rank atom appears in at least one selected activation. In the idealized case where the full-rank features are exactly identified and there is no reconstruction error, the elements of are exactly ground-truth lowrank atoms; with approximate full-rank recovery, they are noisy samples around those atoms. Figure 21: Evolution of feature stability across layers. Left: layer-wise fraction of stable (blue) and unstable (red) SAE features, showing how reproducible and non-reproducible dictionary elements evolve with depth. Right: for stable (blue) and unstable (red) source features separately, the fraction with next-layer decodercosine match at threshold τlayer = 0.7, measuring crosslayer feature matching. decoder-cosine match. G.2 Training-token sweeps: setup and measurement details In Section 7 we study how endpoint stability evolves as SAE training progresses. We sweep the total number of SAE training tokens from 10M to 10B while keeping the SAE architecture and hyperparameters fixed to the main TopK setting (GPT-2, resid_post layer 7, TopK=64, = 214). For each training budget, we train the same number of independent SAEs (different random seeds) and estimate feature reappearance probabilities using the matching procedure in Section 4. We then report the endpoint fractions (unstable and stable) using the same ε and cosine threshold θ as in the main setting. Figure 7 reports the fraction of unstable features as function of training tokens. Figure 22 reports the corresponding stable fraction curve. Together, these curves show that instability decreases early and then approaches non-zero plateau, while the stable fraction continues to increase with diminishing returns. G.3 Automatic interpretation on trained vs. random transformers Figure 23 compares automatic interpretation (detection) scores for SAEs trained on activations from trained GPT-2 versus randomly initialized GPT2 (same SAE architecture and hyperparameters). This shows that high detection scores can occur even in the random-model setting, motivating stability as complementary faithfulness signal. Figure 20: Residual-based recovery of low-rank ground-truth features in the toy model. Cosine similarity between decoder rows and matched ground-truth features before and after replacing unstable decoder rows with centroids obtained from residual-corrected activations. The procedure partially recovers the lowrank block while leaving the already identified full-rank block largely unchanged. We therefore run k-means on with clusters, replace the unstable decoder rows by the resulting centroids, and recompute cosine similarity to the ground-truth dictionary. Figure 20 shows that this substantially improves alignment for the low-rank block in the = 32, = 2, = 8 toy setting. This is diagnostic rather than practical training method: it relies on the toy setups known number of low-rank atoms and selected one-unstable examples, and freezing the replaced decoder rows followed by encoder retraining did not improve EV.

sections.title: G Additional Details for Other Setups
sections.content: G.1 Endpoint fractions across models, layers, and dictionary sizes Section 7 summarizes how endpoint stability varies across base models, layers, and dictionary sizes for TopK SAEs. Table 6 reports the full endpoint fractions for all configurations considered. The left panel of Figure 21 reports the layerwise fractions of stable and unstable features in TopK SAEs with = 64 and = 214, trained on GPT2 residual-stream activations, using 21 independently trained SAEs per layer. The right panel of Figure 21 presents the fraction of stable and unstable source features with next-layer decodercosine match at threshold τlayer = 0.7. Overall, Figure 21 shows that stable SAE features become more prevalent with layer depth and are much more likely than unstable features to admit next-layer 20 Table 6: Fractions of unstable (ˆp ε) and stable (ˆp 1 ε) features for TopK SAEs across dictionary sizes and layers. Figure 23: Automatic interpretation scores for SAEs trained on trained vs. random base models. High detection scores are achievable even on random-model activations, despite the absence of cross-seed reproducibility.
